scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch is commenting on Plato's 4th-century BCE Athenian dialogue, using norms of conduct between citizens and foreigners discussed therein.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The work analyzes Plato's Timaeus (c. -360 to -347); the date -353 is the midpoint of this work-level span.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to Zeno of Citium's pedagogical advice within the context of Athenian intellectual history and theatre culture.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The work focuses on the mid-4th century BCE Athenian philosophical milieu; -353 is the midpoint of the work's span.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This polemic documents the schools of Epicurus and Metrodorus, focusing on their 4th/3rd-century BCE Athenian context.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Epicurus established his school in Athens in -306, initiating the era of Epicurean doctrine discussed by Plutarch.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text discusses the social practices of Greek-born men and the manumission of slaves in the context of Hellenistic philosophical debates.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Midpoint of the work's past-referring span (-341 to 100), covering the historical development of the Epicurean school.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule involves eligibility for public office (magistracy) as debated by Epicurus and Democritus in the Greek world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the arrival of Epicurus in Athens (-306), whose rejection of public office is the subject of the polemic.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the liturgical and social expectations of the wealthy in Greek cities, particularly centered on Athenian and Hellenistic practice.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Midpoint of the work's span (-341 to 100), representing the long-standing Hellenistic tradition of elite public financing.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim specifically highlights the act of Ion of Chios distributing wine to every Athenian citizen after his victory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Ion of Chios was active in the 5th century BCE; -450 represents a central date for his floruit and victories in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule cites the injunctions of Solon regarding the dining rights in the Prytaneum.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Solon's archonship and his era of legislative reform are traditionally dated to -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly describes the local laws and customs of the Prytaneum in the city of Naucratis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Athenaeus is documenting contemporary or slightly earlier practices of his own era and native region around 200 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule cites Macareus's third book on Coan Affairs regarding the exclusion of slaves from Juno's temple.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Macareus of Cos likely wrote in the 4th or 3rd century BCE; -350 represents the midpoint of the Classical/early Hellenistic era for Cos.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns a cult custom in Rhodes regarding the sacrifice of Phorbas.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This represents a traditional cult practice from the classical period of the Rhodian poleis.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to an ancient Roman law prohibiting wine consumption for slaves, women, and the young.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the quasi-legendary and early Republican legal period of Rome, such as the era of the Twelve Tables (c. -450).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text cites Polybius (2nd c. BCE) regarding the prohibition of wine for Roman women.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Polybius's floruit and his description of Roman social institutions peak around -150.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes an ancient Roman restriction on youth until age thirty, as documented by antiquarian sources.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Anchored to the era of early Republican Roman law and traditional social discipline.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the mandatory contributions for the Phiditium (syssitia) in Laconia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the peak period of the classical Spartan 'Lycurgan' constitution and communal dining system.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the decree of Sophocles to banish philosophers from Attica in -307 BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Decree of Sophocles was passed in 307 BCE during the restoration of democracy under Demetrius Poliorcetes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates' work concerns the legal defense of Alcibiades and Athenian judicial procedure.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech was written by Isocrates for Alcibiades' son shortly after his coming of age, roughly 397 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes the performance of liturgies (public financial duties) by a prominent Athenian citizen.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Alcibiades was at the height of his influence and liturgical contributions before the Sicilian Expedition in -415.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Specifically refers to the Amnesty of 403 BCE following the expulsion of the Thirty Tyrants.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Amnesty was a specific historical agreement ratified in -403 to end the Athenian civil war.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the Athenian legal status of 'atimia' (disfranchisement) for citizens unable to pay debts to the state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'Concerning the Team of Horses' is dated to approximately -397 BCE, reflecting contemporary law.
